# Spec: bake FSRS sane defaults into every generated `.apkg`

**Outcome**: Every deck generated by 2anki imports into Anki with sensible FSRS preset weights pre-applied, so the first session feels normal instead of producing the "why is my next review in 17 months" panic that fills r/Anki today. Leading indicator: week-1 review retention rate on freshly converted decks, measured against the current baseline.

**Goal alignment**: Anki out-of-the-box drops new users into a 100-knob configuration panel they did not ask for; the r/Anki signal is unambiguous (8+ threads in a 50-thread sweep are variants of "I don't understand FSRS"). 2anki controls the deck file, not the user's collection — but if we can embed a working FSRS preset in the `.apkg` itself, every converted deck becomes a "just works" experience. Simpler, faster, and a clear differentiator against raw Anki — and a moat AnkiDroid's GSoC 2026 prompt-to-deck generator does not address.

## Problem

A beginner converts a Notion page or a Quizlet set on 2anki, downloads the `.apkg`, imports it in Anki Desktop, and starts reviewing. Anki applies the user's collection-level FSRS defaults — which on a fresh profile are the FSRS optimizer's "general" weights, but only after the user has opted in via the deck options menu most beginners never find. The first interval looks alien; the user posts to r/Anki; some never come back.

We cannot reach into the user's collection from outside, but the `.apkg` format carries a sqlite database, and the `col.dconf` row in that database is where FSRS preset weights live. If we write that row at generation time, the import path *may* apply our preset to the imported notes. The "may" is the entire feature risk.

## Riskiest assumption

That modern Anki actually picks up a `col.dconf` preset embedded in an externally-generated `.apkg` and applies it to the imported notes. FSRS configuration is per-profile in Anki's data model, not per-deck. It is plausible Anki ignores foreign `dconf` rows on import for exactly this reason, in which case the entire feature is a no-op no matter how well we write the sqlite.

## Phase 0: feasibility spike (required, ~4-6 hours)

**Spike deliverable**: take an existing 2anki-generated `.apkg`, post-process the embedded sqlite to insert a `col.dconf` row with chosen FSRS preset weights, import it in current Anki Desktop and AnkiMobile, and confirm Anki uses the preset for the imported notes' next-review calculation.

**Decision rule**: if either client ignores the preset on import, **kill the build phase**. Drop the feature, write a 200-word `/fsrs-explained` SEO page instead (still captures the same search intent), and document the spike result so we do not re-run it in six months.

This spike does not require a code change in this repo. It needs:
- One generated `.apkg` from prod.
- A short script that opens the zip, opens the sqlite, inserts a `dconf` row, re-zips.
- A real Anki Desktop install + a Mac/iOS or Android device for the mobile check.

The spike runs before any TypeScript or Python is written for Phase 1.

## Phase 1: build (only if spike passes)

**Pipeline change**: `write_apkg.py` continues to use `genanki.Deck` and `genanki.Package` as today. After `package.write_to_file(...)` returns, post-process the resulting zip:

1. Open the `.apkg` zip in-place.
2. Open the embedded `collection.anki2` sqlite.
3. Insert/update the `col.dconf` JSON row with the chosen FSRS preset weights.
4. Repack the zip atomically (write to `<file>.tmp`, fsync, rename).

**Preset source**: the FSRS optimizer's published "general" weights, or the FSRS Helper add-on's documented default — whichever is the canonical reference at spike time. **Do not invent weights.** Cite the source in the implementation commit.

**No UI**: this is invisible-by-design. No setting, no toggle, no per-deck override. One preset for everyone, baked in.

## Scope

- **In:** post-processing `.apkg` zips to embed a `col.dconf` row with FSRS preset weights, on every conversion path that emits a deck.
- **Out:** per-user FSRS optimization (runs against the user's review history, not ours); per-deck custom weights; a UI knob for the user to override the preset; modifying any other `col.*` row.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] **Phase 0 spike**: a written one-page result (in this PR's body or a `/Documentation/decisions/` note) confirming whether Anki Desktop and AnkiMobile honor an externally-supplied `col.dconf` preset on import. Spike result determines whether Phase 1 runs at all.
- [ ] **Phase 1, if green**: a sample `.apkg` generated by the pipeline visibly uses the FSRS preset after import in Anki Desktop (next-review intervals match preset expectations on synthetic test data).
- [ ] Existing conversion tests still pass.
- [ ] New `*.test.ts` or `*.test.py` covers the zip post-processing step in isolation (open zip, mutate sqlite, repack, re-read).
- [ ] No changelog entry on the spike-only commit. Phase 1 ships its own changelog entry: one line, user voice, e.g. "Decks now import with sensible review intervals out of the box."

## Risks

- **Anki version drift.** The `col.dconf` schema is internal to Anki and has changed across 2.1.x releases. Pin the spike to a specific Anki version; document it; recheck on Anki major-version bumps.
- **No-op feature.** Per the riskiest assumption — the spike exists to find this out for 4-6 hours of work instead of 4-6 days.
- **Repacking the zip atomically** — must not corrupt the `.apkg` mid-write. Existing `lib/zip` helpers already handle this pattern for ingestion; reuse rather than reinvent.

## Out of scope

- Per-user FSRS optimization on our servers (would require the user's review history, which we do not have).
- Replacing `genanki` with a lower-level apkg writer.
- Surfacing FSRS settings in the 2anki UI.
- Bundling the FSRS Helper add-on or any other add-on with the deck.
